trajectory_summary: This is a pure logistics call — a missed appointment re-slotted with no re-engagement of the customer's original motivation. The appointment is booked and the customer is cooperative, but Holland never surfaced why the customer wanted solar in the first place, never anchored bill pain, and never created any curiosity to carry the customer through to Thursday. The closer will walk into an unprimed room with no established emotional foundation. The fragility here is structural — inferred from what was omitted, not from any affirmative signal of doubt from the customer.
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag is unknown, but the appointment was booked. That said, 'booked' overstates the quality of this outcome. The re-booking is structurally fragile: no motivation re-anchor, no bill pain surfaced, no curiosity created, and the button-up was incomplete (no 'is that fair' handshake, no computer/no-driving anchor per button_up required elements). A booked-but-unprimed appointment on a recycled lead carries meaningful no-show risk.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: we were supposed to have a phone call with you, actually, yesterday to show you that solar estimate, but we weren't able to reach you. And so we're just looking to get you
primary_coaching_focus.why: Holland treated this entire call as a scheduling transaction. On a recycled lead where the customer's interest has cooled since the original inquiry, the re-anchor is the job — not just the re-slot. Holland never asked why the customer was interested in solar, never surfaced bill pain, and never created any curiosity to carry the customer to Thursday. The closer will inherit an appointment with no emotional foundation. Manager should work with Holland on a one-sentence motivation question to insert before the scheduling ask on every reschedule call.

key_moments.quote: we were supposed to have a phone call with you, actually, yesterday to show you that solar estimate, but we weren't able to reach you. And so we're just looking to get you
key_moments.stewart_read: Holland opens a reschedule call by explaining the missed connection — a logistics frame — rather than re-establishing legitimacy and the customer's original motivation. On a recycled/reschedule touch, the intro beat still applies: the customer's interest has cooled since the original inquiry, and the first 30 seconds are the window to re-warm it. Holland skips this entirely. Strategic read: Holland may be deliberately keeping it brief to avoid re-opening objections on a cooperative customer. Reactive read: Holland defaulted to logistics because there was no script for reschedule re-engagement. The reactive read is more supported — there is no evidence of a deliberate strategy to re-anchor later in the call.

key_moments.quote: we were just looking to to see if we can get you back on this schedule just because the specialist has some calls going on today. So I don't think you would have time today, but we could totally schedule for tomorrow if you have some time.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the natural moment to briefly surface bill pain before asking for the new slot — 'before we find a time, I just want to make sure we're still set up to show you what your bill could look like' — but Holland goes straight to availability. The bill anchor is never touched in this call. Per the codex, bill capture and emotional flip are required work; on a reschedule call where the bill may already be on file, the flip is the remaining job. Holland does neither.

key_moments.quote: Or I could do, like, a lunchtime sometime.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the single most active moment of customer engagement in the call. The customer volunteers a scheduling preference unprompted — they are working to make the appointment happen. This is a small but real signal that the customer still has some residual interest. Holland uses it only for scheduling logistics. The coaching opportunity: when a customer leans in to make an appointment work, that is the crack to briefly surface their original motivation — 'I love that you want to make this work — just so we're set up well for Thursday, can I ask what was it about your bill that got you interested in the first place?' One sentence. Then confirm the slot.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. So Thursday at 01:30, and then it will just be a phone call. So if you could just have, like, access to your email during the call, just that way he can email you over all the info.
key_moments.stewart_read: Holland's button-up is incomplete. The email-access ask is present, but the required elements are missing: no computer anchor ('you'll be able to sit down to review the design on your computer'), no no-driving requirement, and no 'is that fair' verbal handshake. Per the codex, the handshake is predictive of show rate and should not be skipped. The email mention also introduces a potential expectation gap — the customer may expect to receive the proposal by email before the call, which conflicts with the hard rule in rebuttals.send_proposal_before_meeting.
